title: Dhalpuri roti
subtitle: Trinidadian flatbread stuffed with spiced yellow split peas
excerpt: Soft Trinidadian flatbread wrapped around a filling of ground yellow split peas seasoned with cumin and garlic. The most celebrated roti in Trinidad, eaten wrapped around curried fillings.
sourceNotes: Dhalpuri roti is the most prized of the Trinidadian rotis, distinguished by its split pea filling which gives each flatbread a speckled yellow interior and a nuttier flavour than plain roti. It is descended from Indian flatbread traditions brought by indentured workers in the nineteenth century. The split peas are ground to a coarse meal after boiling, seasoned with cumin and garlic, then rolled inside the dough and cooked on a tawa (a flat iron griddle). A roti pan or flat cast-iron pan serves well in place of a tawa.

content.text: Dhalpuri is the roti you wrap around curry goat or curry chicken in Trinidad. The split pea filling gives it more structure than a plain roti and more flavour. The process takes a couple of hours but is not difficult. The main thing is keeping the split pea filling dry. If it is too wet, it will burst through the dough when you roll it.

The dough is a simple unleavened flour dough; the split peas are boiled until tender, then coarsely ground with cumin and garlic. The filling goes inside the dough ball, which is sealed and then rolled flat. Cook on a dry, very hot griddle, the surface should have a few small charred spots.

Rinse the {{split-peas-yellow}} of yellow split peas and put them in a saucepan with enough cold water to cover by 5 cm. Bring to a boil, then simmer for 20 to 25 minutes until just tender but not mushy. Drain well. While still warm, process the split peas in a food processor with the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt until a coarse, grainy paste forms. The mixture should be dry enough to hold its shape. Spread on a plate to cool.

Combine the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{baking-powder}} of baking powder, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt in a large bowl. Add the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil and rub in with your fingers. Add 220 to 240 ml of warm water, a little at a time, and mix to a smooth, soft dough. Knead for 3 minutes. Cover and rest for 30 minutes.

Divide the dough into 8 equal balls. Flatten each ball into a small round, place a heaped tablespoon of the split pea filling in the centre, bring the edges of the dough up around the filling, and pinch firmly to seal. Place sealed-side down and leave to rest for 5 minutes. On a lightly floured surface, roll each stuffed ball into a thin round about 22 cm in diameter, roll gently and evenly to avoid the filling breaking through. Heat a dry roti pan or cast-iron pan over high heat until very hot. Cook each roti for 1 to 2 minutes per side, pressing the surface lightly with a folded cloth to encourage even contact, until light golden spots appear on both sides. Rub a little oil over each roti as it comes off the pan.

items.fix: The paste must be completely dry before filling. Drain the split peas very well and cook the paste in a dry pan for a minute to drive off any remaining moisture. Roll slowly and evenly outward from the centre.
items.cause: Split pea paste too wet; rolling too fast

items.fix: The dough should be soft and pliable. Add water a little more than seems right. Cook each side for only 1 to 2 minutes, the roti should have spots, not be uniformly brown.
Toast the cumin seeds in a dry pan for 30 seconds before grinding. The filling needs generous salt to flavour the plain split peas.
The pan must be fully preheated before the first roti goes on. A drop of water should evaporate immediately on contact.
items.cause: Pan not hot enough at the start

content.text: Dhalpuri roti is central to Indo-Trinidadian cooking, and it has spread across all communities in Trinidad. Roti shops in Trinidad and in the Trinidadian diaspora sell dhalpuri alongside busup shut (torn paratha), with curries ladled inside. The name comes from the Hindi dhal (lentils or split peas) and puri (a type of fried bread). In Trinidad, dhalpuri is griddled rather than fried. The dish reflects the history of Indian indenture in the Caribbean and the cooking traditions that took root and changed over time.
